title: Ribbon-tied single-section binding
subtitle: A pamphlet-stitch variant that closes with a ribbon bow rather than a knot, a useful starting structure for handmade gifts
excerpt: The ribbon-tied binding sews a single-section booklet and replaces the traditional thread knot with a ribbon drawn through the spine and tied in a bow on the outside. The ribbon becomes both the fastener and a decorative element. Closure strength is sufficient for notebooks under thirty leaves.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on simple pamphlet sewing. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock. The ribbon variation is a standard domestic adaptation of the pamphlet structure documented in Arts-and-Crafts-era bookbinding literature.

glossaryTerms.definition: A single-signature sewing method using three or five holes and one length of thread. The simplest practical binding stitch, one thread, one needle, one knot.
The simplest knot: pass the working end over and through the loop once. Used in bookbinding to start a thread before sewing.

content.text: uses one thread through three or five holes in a single folded section. In the ribbon variation, the thread is replaced for the central hole with a narrower ribbon, which exits the spine and ties in a bow on the outside. The structural thread remains, linen at the two outer stations, and the ribbon adds a closure that holds the book shut without a clasp or band.

For the ribbon: 4-6 mm wide grosgrain or satin ribbon, cut to approximately three times the book's spine height. The ribbon piercing hole at the centre should be sized to the ribbon width, use the awl to elongate the hole slightly with two or three rocking passes.
Fold ten to twenty-five sheets of 90 gsm cartridge in half with the bone folder. Nest them inside a cover of 240 gsm card or heavy paper, folded to match.
Mark five piercing points on the spine: one 10 mm from the head, one 10 mm from the tail, one at the centre, and two between each of these.

Pierce holes at the head and tail stations, and at the two intermediate stations, with the bookbinding awl. For the centre station, widen the hole to accept the ribbon using two rocking passes with the awl point.
Thread the needle with a length of waxed linen roughly four times the spine height. Start inside the section at the head station. Pass out through the head hole, leaving a 40 mm tail inside.

Pass in through the second station, out through the third, and in again toward the centre. Keep the thread on the inside at all middle stations; only the head and tail stations have thread on the outside.

At the centre station: pass the needle through but do not pull tight yet. Feed the cut ribbon end through the centre hole from the outside, using the needle as a guide if needed. Pull both the thread and the ribbon ends until they are snug.
Continue the thread to the tail, working the same stitch pattern. Tie the working end to the starting tail inside the section with a square knot, not an
, which works loose. Trim thread ends to 10 mm.
On the outside, centre the ribbon so equal lengths emerge from each side of the spine. The book closes when the ribbon is tied in a bow; the bow sits on the spine in the traditional manner.

items.symptom: The ribbon pulls through the spine when the book is closed
items.cause: The centre hole is too large or the ribbon too narrow for the hole.
items.fix: Use a ribbon at least 4 mm wide and keep the centre hole tight. The ribbon should need a firm pull to pass through, not fall through freely.

items.symptom: The thread shows through the cover on the outside
items.cause: The intermediate sewing stations are too close to the spine edge.
items.fix: Keep all sewing stations except the outer two at least 5 mm inside the fold line. The linen thread running across the inside of the fold should not be visible from the cover when the book is closed.

items.symptom: The overhand knot on the ribbon loosens over time
items.cause: Synthetic ribbon does not hold knots as firmly as grosgrain.
items.fix: Grosgrain ribbon has a textured surface that grips knots. Satin ribbon is more prone to slipping; pass the ribbon through a small metal ring sewn at each cover edge to stop it pulling free entirely.
